title: The Eye in the Abyss
content:
The net screamed.

Hai Ping had heard that sound a thousand times before—the hemp ropes groaning against the pull of a full catch, the wooden hull of the fishing skiff whimpering under strain. But this was different. This was the sound of something being asked to hold more than it could bear.

"Father!" He grabbed the rope, felt the fibers biting into his calloused palms. "There's something—"

"Let go!" His father's voice cut through the morning mist, sharp as a blade. "Let it go, boy!"

But Hai Ping couldn't. His hands wouldn't open. The rope was already moving, whipping across the deck, pulling him toward the rail. He dug his heels into the weathered planks, felt them splinter under his weight.

The skiff tilted.

Water flooded over the side, cold and black, swallowing his ankles, his knees. His father was shouting something—his name, maybe, or a prayer—but the sea had found its rhythm now, a deep pulse that vibrated through the hull like a heartbeat.

Hai Ping went over.

The cold hit him first. Not a shock, but a possession. It climbed into his bones, wrapped around his lungs, squeezed until he forgot how to breathe. He opened his mouth to scream and the sea drank his voice.

Below him, the rope vanished into darkness.

No. Not darkness.

The light came from somewhere impossibly deep, a blue-green glow that shouldn't exist at this depth, a color that had no name in any language humans spoke. It pulsed. Slow. Steady. Like something breathing.

Hai Ping's chest burned. He needed air. He needed to surface, to find his father, to pretend this was just a nightmare born from too much salt wind and not enough sleep.

But his hands wouldn't let go.

The rope pulled him deeper.

He'd been raised on stories. Every fisherman's son in the village of Yù Lóng was. His grandmother had filled his childhood with tales of dragons that controlled the rain, of phoenixes that rose from ashes made of starlight, of mountains that walked and rivers that sang. But the stories she told most often were about the Kun Peng.

*At the bottom of the Northern Ocean,* she would whisper, her hands working the endless thread of her embroidery, *there sleeps a fish so vast that the shadows of its fins create the seasons. When it dreams of flying, the currents change. When it breathes, the tides obey.*

Hai Ping had always thought it was just a story.

The rope stopped.

He hung there, suspended in the abyss, his lungs screaming, his vision narrowing to a single point of light below him. The glow was closer now. Brighter. It filled his whole world, pushing back the darkness until he could see—

The eye opened.

It was not like any eye he had ever seen. It was not like any eye a human mind could comprehend. It was a universe contained in a single curve of gelatinous flesh, a galaxy of gold and black and ancient light that had been watching the world turn for ten thousand years.

No. Longer.

The eye looked at him.

And Hai Ping understood, in that moment, that everything he had ever known was a lie painted on the surface of a deeper truth. The sea was not water. It was the creature's breath. The sky was not air. It was the space between the creature's dreams. The mountains on the horizon were not stone. They were the curve of its back, breaking the surface of a world too small to hold it.

The eye spoke.

Not in words. There was no sound, no vibration in the water, no movement of lips or tongue. The voice came from inside Hai Ping's own skull, a pressure behind his eyes, a resonance in his bones.

*Little one.*

He tried to scream. Nothing came out. His lungs had given up, his body had accepted the impossibility of his situation, and he hung there like a child's forgotten toy, waiting for the end.

*You caught me.*

The net. The impossible weight. The rope that had pulled him from his father's skiff into this place where light and dark meant nothing.

*I am sorry. I did not mean to wake you.*

A rumble. A shift in the pressure around him. The eye blinked—a movement that took years, that rearranged the currents of the entire ocean, that sent ripples across the surface that would become storms in distant lands.

*Wake? Child, I have not slept.*

Hai Ping's mind reeled. The stories said the Kun Peng had slumbered for ten thousand years. That its dreams were the weather. That its awakening would be the end of everything.

*The stories are wrong.*

The voice was patient. Gentle. Like an old man explaining the world to a grandson who could not yet understand.

*I have been waiting.*

For what?

The thought was not his own. Or perhaps it was. In this place, in the presence of something so vast that it bent reality around itself, the line between his mind and the creature's had begun to blur.

*For you.*

The water around him began to move. Not a current, but a pressure, a gathering of force that pushed him upward, toward the surface, toward the light that was not the creature's glow but the sun, finally breaking through the clouds.

*You bear the seal, little one. You carry the mark of the first Keeper. You have been called.*

Hai Ping broke the surface gasping, choking, vomiting seawater into the gray morning air. His father's skiff was fifty yards away, circling, his father's voice raw with desperation.

But Hai Ping could not hear him.

He could only hear the voice, fading now, receding into the depths like a dream at dawn.

*Do not fear. I will not wake. But you must come back. You must learn. The war has begun, little Keeper, and the gods will not wait for you to grow.*

The water stilled.

Hai Ping floated on his back, staring at the sky, feeling the weight of something ancient pressing against his chest. He touched his skin, expecting to find a mark, a brand, some physical proof of what had just happened.

Nothing.

But when he looked at his hands, he saw that the calluses from the fishing ropes had begun to fade. And beneath his skin, faint as starlight through clouds, something gold pulsed in time with the rhythm of the deep.

His father's boat reached him. Strong hands grabbed his arms, pulled him aboard, wrapped him in blankets that smelled of salt and sweat and years of hard work.

"Boy. Boy! Are you hurt? What happened? What did you see?"

Hai Ping looked at his father's face—lined, weathered, full of love and fear—and opened his mouth to answer.

But the words would not come.

Because in the reflection of his father's eyes, he saw something that should not be possible.

The sky had changed.

The clouds had rearranged themselves into a pattern that meant nothing to his father, but everything to Hai Ping. It was a map. A path. A summons written in water vapor and wind.

He turned his head toward the horizon.

In the distance, where the sea met the sky, a mountain had appeared. A mountain that had not been there yesterday. A mountain that rose like a tooth from the jaw of the world, black and jagged and wrong.

And at its peak, a light.

Not a fire. Not a beacon. Something older.

Something waiting.

Hai Ping sat up. His body ached. His lungs burned. His mind was a tangle of impossible images and ancient voices.

But he understood one thing with perfect clarity.

The Kun Peng had not woken.

But something else had.

And it was coming for him.

title: The Weight of Ten Thousand Years
content:
The morning came grey and cold, clouds pressing down on the bay like a lid on a pot.

Hai Ping had not slept. He sat on the rocks where the fishing boats tied up, watching the water. Waiting. His father's boat still hadn't returned, and neither had the others that had gone out before the storm. Three days now. The village elders said prayers at the temple. The women set out offerings of rice wine and steamed buns. The children were told not to ask questions.

But Hai Ping had seen what they hadn't.

The water at the bay's edge had changed. The tide came in faster than it should, and went out slower. The fish had vanished—not just the good ones, but everything. Even the crabs had crawled up onto the beach and died there, shells cracking underfoot. His mother said it was bad luck. His uncle said the ocean was sick.

Hai Ping knew better now.

The dream had come again last night, stronger than before. A voice like stone grinding against stone, speaking words that weren't words. He had seen darkness so deep it hurt to look at, and something moving in it. Something vast. Something that breathed and the breathing made the world tremble.

*Keeper.*

The word sat in his chest like a stone lodged between his ribs.

"You look like death."

Hai Ping turned. Old Chen stood behind him, leaning on his bamboo staff. The man was supposed to be the village's spirit medium, though most people thought he was just crazy. He wore layers of ragged cloth and spoke to the wind. But his eyes—those eyes missed nothing.

"Your father's boat?" Old Chen asked.

"Not yet."

"They won't come back."

Hai Ping's hands tightened on the rock. "You don't know that."

"I know the sea." Old Chen sat down beside him, joints popping. "I know what lives in it. And I know what's waking up."

The old man reached into his robe and pulled out something wrapped in yellow silk. He unwrapped it slowly, reverently. A tablet of dark jade, worn smooth by centuries of touch. Characters were carved into its surface—ancient seal script that Hai Ping couldn't read.

"This belonged to your great-great-grandfather," Old Chen said. "And his grandfather before him. The Keepers always carry the mark."

He took Hai Ping's hand and turned it over. The blue lines on his palm had deepened overnight. They spread now from wrist to fingertips, pulsing faintly like veins filled with seawater.

Old Chen nodded slowly. "You've been chosen."

"Chosen for what?"

"To carry a burden no man should carry." The old man pressed the jade tablet into Hai Ping's hands. "The Kun Peng sleeps because someone must keep it asleep. That someone has always been your bloodline. But the seal is breaking, boy. The gods are fighting again, and they want the great beast as their weapon."

Hai Ping stared at the tablet. The characters seemed to shift as he watched them, rearranging themselves into patterns that almost made sense.

"I don't understand."

"No. You don't." Old Chen stood, his joints cracking again. "But you will. Come with me."

They walked along the cliff path that led to the old shrine—a place Hai Ping had avoided his whole life. The villagers said it was haunted. They said people who went there didn't come back the same. Hai Ping had always thought they meant ghosts.

Now he wondered if they meant something worse.

The shrine sat at the edge of a cliff, half its foundation crumbling into the sea below. Wind howled through broken windows. Salt had eaten the paint from the wooden beams, leaving them grey and splintered. But the door was new. Oak, iron-bound, with a lock that gleamed like it had been forged yesterday.

Old Chen produced a key from around his neck. "Inside is everything your family has learned about the Kun Peng. Writings, maps, rituals. Your father was supposed to teach you, but he thought he had more time."

"More time for what?"

"For the war to reach us." The lock clicked open. "It's here now."

The door swung inward, and Hai Ping smelled dust and old incense and something else—something that made his skin prickle. Copper. Like blood.

The shrine's interior was larger than it should have been. The walls were covered in scrolls, hundreds of them, stacked in wooden racks that reached the ceiling. A statue of some creature dominated the center—half fish, half bird, its stone wings spread as if to catch the wind. Its eyes were closed, but Hai Ping felt them watching him.

On the altar beneath the statue sat a bowl of water. Clear, still, perfect.

Old Chen gestured. "Look."

Hai Ping approached the bowl. Inside, the water showed not the shrine's ceiling, but the ocean depths. He saw darkness, and in that darkness, something that made his breath catch.

A creature so vast it filled the water like a mountain fills the sky. Scales the size of islands. An eye the length of a ship, half-lidded, dreaming. And around it, chains of light—golden seals that flickered and dimmed like candles in a storm.

"The Kun Peng," Old Chen said. "Bound by the First Keeper ten thousand years ago. Waking slowly now, because the gods are idiots who can't stop fighting long enough to remember why they put it to sleep in the first place."

"Why?"

"Because when it wakes, it will rise. When it rises, it will become a bird. And when it becomes a bird, its wings will push the ocean away from the bottom of the world." The old man's voice dropped. "Every coastal city from here to the ends of the earth will drown. Billions will die. And the gods won't care, because they'll be too busy using the Kun Peng to murder each other."

Hai Ping stared at the dreaming eye in the water. It was beautiful. Horrifying. And somehow, impossibly, familiar.

"I dreamed of it," he whispered.

"You are bonded to it, boy. You feel what it feels. When it stirs, you stir. When it dreams, you dream." Old Chen touched Hai Ping's shoulder. "And when it wakes, you will wake with it."

"Then stop it."

"I can't. I'm just the messenger." The old man's face softened. "But you—you are the Keeper. The seal is tied to your blood. You can strengthen it. You can calm the beast. You can send it back to sleep."

"How?"

Old Chen pointed at the wall of scrolls. "Start reading."

Hai Ping turned. There were thousands of scrolls, each one covered in dense characters that swam before his eyes. Some were so old the ink had faded to nothing. Others were stained with water, torn by insects, crumbling to dust.

"I can't read all of this."

"You have three weeks."

"Three weeks for what?"

"Until the celestial army arrives." Old Chen's voice was flat, matter-of-fact. "The Jade Emperor's forces are marching south. They intend to claim the Kun Peng by force. If they reach the seal before you strengthen it, they'll break it themselves and take the beast as their war mount."

"Then stop them."

"I told you. I'm just a messenger." The old man shuffled toward the door. "But I'll give you one piece of advice. The Kun Peng is not a monster. It's not a weapon. It's a creature that has been asleep for ten thousand years, dreaming of the sky it used to fly through. If you want to calm it, you need to understand that. You need to feel what it feels."

Hai Ping looked back at the water bowl. The great eye was still closed, still dreaming.

"How?"

"By going to it."

The words hung in the air.

"Go to it?" Hai Ping's voice cracked. "It's at the bottom of the ocean. Miles down. I'll be crushed."

"No." Old Chen stopped at the door. "You're the Keeper. The seal protects you. It will let you breathe, let you survive the pressure, let you walk where no mortal can walk."

"You want me to jump into the ocean and swim to a creature the size of a mountain range?"

"I want you to do whatever it takes to keep the Kun Peng asleep." The old man's eyes were hard. "Because if you don't, everyone you love will die. Your mother. Your sisters. The fisherman's daughter you've been too shy to talk to for three years. All of them."

Hai Ping's jaw tightened. "I don't know how."

"Then learn." Old Chen stepped through the door. "The library is yours. The seal is yours. The burden is yours. I've done my part."

"Wait."

The old man paused.

"If I go to it," Hai Ping said slowly, "if I dive down to the Kun Peng... what happens if I fail?"

Old Chen was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was barely a whisper.

"Then the world ends, and you get to watch."

He closed the door. The lock clicked. Hai Ping was alone.

He stood in the center of the shrine, surrounded by ten thousand years of his family's knowledge, the jade tablet heavy in his hands, the blue veins pulsing under his skin.

And in the water bowl, the great eye opened.

Hai Ping felt it like a punch to the chest. The Kun Peng was awake. Not fully—just enough to notice him. Just enough to turn its dreaming attention toward the tiny human who carried its seal.

*Keeper.*

The voice wasn't sound. It was pressure. It was the weight of the ocean pressing against his bones.

*Come.*

The water in the bowl rippled. The surface split. A path opened—not in the bowl, but in the air itself. A tunnel of shimmering salt water that stretched from the shrine floor into darkness.

Hai Ping's heart hammered. His hands shook. Every instinct screamed at him to run, to hide, to pretend none of this was real.

But his feet moved forward.

And the tunnel closed behind him.

title: The First Test of the Seal
content:
The bone boat drifted through water so dark it swallowed the moon's reflection.

Hai Ping's fingers had gone numb around the oar. He hadn't rowed in hours. Didn't need to. The current pulled them deeper into the trench, past cliffs of black stone where pale fish with no eyes swam in lazy spirals. His father's compass sat heavy in his pocket, the needle spinning in slow, useless circles.

"Ai." Old Chen spat over the side. "Lost already."

"You said you knew this passage."

"I know the passage that was here last winter." The old man's face looked like cracked leather in the dim light. "The seafloor moves. Shifts. The gods don't keep maps the way men do."

Hai Ping wanted to argue. Wanted to demand they turn back, find the surface, forget this entire nightmare. But the truth sat cold in his chest — he couldn't go home. The village wouldn't take him back. The magistrate's men would drag him to the capital in chains, and the scholars would cut him open to see what made a fisherman's son speak with the voice of a god.

The Kun Peng had marked him.

He touched the seal on his chest. The skin felt warm, wrong, like a fever that wouldn't break.

"How much further?"

Old Chen shrugged. "Could be a mile. Could be we've already passed it. The entrance to the Sea Palace moves because the Queen doesn't want visitors." He squinted at Hai Ping. "But she'll want you."

"Why?"

"Because you're the first Keeper in three hundred years. Because the last one nearly sank the Eastern Continent when he lost his temper." The old man's laugh came out like rocks grinding together. "The Queen has been waiting for a new leash for the beast. And here you come, walking right into her hand."

The water changed.

Hai Ping felt it before he saw it — a shift in pressure, like diving too deep too fast. The hair on his arms stood up. The bone boat shuddered, and somewhere below, something groaned.

"What was that?"

Old Chen's face went white. "She knows we're here."

The seafloor beneath them split open.

Light erupted from the crack — blue and cold and ancient, the color of lightning trapped in ice. The bone boat spun, caught in a whirlpool that hadn't existed a moment ago. Hai Ping grabbed the sides, felt the bones grinding against each other, threatening to break apart.

"Hold on!" Old Chen grabbed the oar, tried to steer, but the current had them. It pulled them down, into the crack, into the light, and Hai Ping closed his eyes as the world turned upside down.

When he opened them, they were floating in air.

The boat hung suspended in a cavern so vast it had no ceiling. Pillars of jade rose from a floor of polished obsidian, each one carved with creatures that moved — serpents that coiled and uncoiled, birds that spread their wings and folded them again, beasts with too many eyes that watched the boat drift past. Light came from nowhere and everywhere, pale and directionless, like being inside a cloud.

"The Sea Queen's court." Old Chen's voice had gone quiet. Respectful. Scared. "We're in her domain now."

The boat settled on the obsidian floor with a sound like a sigh. Hai Ping stepped off, felt solid ground beneath his feet for the first time in what felt like days. His legs shook. His head spun. The seal on his chest burned hot, then cold, then hot again.

"She's calling you."

"I don't hear anything."

"You will."

The pillars stretched ahead, forming a corridor that led to a throne made of coral and bone. Empty. But Hai Ping could feel the presence in the room, heavy as water pressure, pressing against his skull from all sides.

"Queen of the Eastern Sea." Old Chen bowed so low his forehead touched the ground. "This humble fisherman brings the Keeper, as commanded."

Silence.

Then laughter.

It came from everywhere — from the pillars, from the floor, from the air itself. A woman's laugh, musical and terrible, the sound of waves breaking on rocks. The throne shimmered, and a figure materialized on it, draped in robes of living seaweed that moved like they had their own will.

The Sea Queen was beautiful in the way drowning is beautiful.

Her skin glowed pale blue. Her hair flowed around her like ink in water, and her eyes — her eyes held no whites, only black, only depths, only the endless dark of the ocean floor.

"Look at you." Her voice wrapped around him, soft and suffocating. "A child. A fisherman's child. The heavens send me a boy with salt on his hands and call it a solution."

Hai Ping forced himself to meet her gaze. "I didn't ask for this."

"No one asks for anything worth having." She leaned forward, and the seaweed robes pooled around her like captured waves. "Do you know what you carry, boy? Do you understand the weight of the seal on your chest?"

"A bond. With the Kun Peng."

"A leash." She spat the word. "A cage made of flesh and blood. The Celestial Emperor bound the beast to a human host so it could never wake fully, never rise, never challenge his throne." Her lips curled. "And now the Emperor sits in his golden palace, safe from the creature he fears, while mortal children burn from the inside carrying his chains."

Hai Ping's hand went to his chest. The seal pulsed. Throbbed. Like a heartbeat that wasn't his.

"If I'm bonded to the Kun Peng, then I can control it. Calm it. Keep it asleep."

"You can try." The Queen stood, and the room darkened. "But the Kun Peng is not a dog to be soothed with kind words and gentle hands. It is the largest thing that has ever lived. Older than your Emperor. Older than my seas. Older than the mountains and the stars and the gods themselves." She walked toward him, and each step sent ripples through the air. "When it dreams, continents shift. When it stirs, tidal waves swallow islands. And when it wakes — "

She stopped inches from him. Close enough that he could smell salt and rot and something ancient, something that had been sleeping at the bottom of the ocean since before humanity learned to walk.

"When it wakes, the world ends."

Hai Ping's throat went dry. "Then why would anyone want it to wake?"

"Because endings can be beginnings. Because the Celestial Emperor has ruled for ten thousand years, and there are those who would see his reign end." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "Because I am one of them."

The seal on his chest exploded with pain.

Hai Ping screamed. Fell to his knees. The seal burned through his shirt, through his skin, into his bones, and he could see it — not with his eyes but with something deeper — the shape of the creature below, the Kun Peng sleeping in the trench, its body stretching for miles, its breath creating currents that circled the world.

And something was wrong.

The Kun Peng wasn't sleeping peacefully.

It was thrashing.

Dreaming of fire. Dreaming of war. Dreaming of rising from the depths and spreading wings that would blot out the sun.

"No." Hai Ping gritted his teeth. "No, no, no — "

"You feel it." The Queen's voice came from far away. "The celestial armies have engaged above. The Emperor's forces and the rebels clash in the heavens, and their battle shakes the foundations of the world. The Kun Peng feels the violence. It wants to wake."

"I can't — "

"You must calm it. Now. Or the seal will break, and everything dies."

Hai Ping closed his eyes. Dove into the bond. Found himself falling through dark water, past fish that glowed with their own light, past wrecks of ships from centuries he didn't recognize, down and down and down until he reached the bottom.

And there it was.

The Kun Peng.

He couldn't comprehend its size. His mind refused. It was like trying to look at a mountain from an inch away — he could only see pieces, scales each the size of villages, fins that stretched further than he could see, an eye as large as the moon that slowly, slowly opened.

The eye was ancient. Tired. Angry.

And it was looking at him.

*Little keeper.*

The voice shook his soul. Rattled his teeth. Filled every corner of his consciousness until there was nothing else.

*They fight above. They shake my home. They wake me from dreams I have dreamed for ten thousand years.*

"Please." Hai Ping's voice came out small. "Please stay asleep. If you wake — "

*If I wake, I end them. All of them. The Emperor who chained me. The rebels who disturb my rest. The gods and their petty wars.* The eye blinked, slow as glaciers. *And you, little keeper. You would end too.*

"I know."

Another blink. The Kun Peng shifted, and the ocean floor cracked for a hundred miles.

*You would die to stop me.*

"I would die to save my village. My father. The people who fish the same waters you sleep in." Hai Ping's voice steadied. "I didn't ask for this bond. I don't want it. But I have it, and I won't let you destroy everything because you're angry at gods who don't care about any of us."

Silence.

The Kun Peng's eye watched him. Studied him. And slowly, so slowly, the creature's massive form settled. The thrashing stopped. The currents calmed.

*You have courage, little keeper.*

"People keep saying that. I think it's just stupidity."

The Kun Peng's laugh was an earthquake. *Perhaps. But courage and stupidity are often the same thing, seen from different angles.*

The eye began to close.

*They will test you. The Queen. The Emperor. All of them. They will try to use you to control me, or wake me, or kill me. You must choose your path carefully, little keeper.*

"What path should I choose?"

*The one that keeps me asleep.* The eye closed fully. *Because if I wake again, I will not go back to sleep. And the world will drown.*

The vision shattered.

Hai Ping gasped, found himself on his knees in the Sea Queen's court, Old Chen's hands on his shoulders, the Queen's black eyes boring into him.

"The seal held." Her voice carried surprise. And something else. Something like respect. "You calmed it."

"I talked to it."

"Impossible. No Keeper has spoken with the Kun Peng in three thousand years."

Hai Ping pulled himself to his feet. His legs shook. His chest ached. But he met her eyes and didn't look away.

"I talked to it. And it listened." He drew a breath. "You said you want to use me against the Emperor. But I'm not your weapon. I'm not anyone's weapon."

The Queen's expression flickered. "Then what are you?"

Hai Ping touched the seal on his chest. Felt the warmth of the creature sleeping below, the weight of an entire ocean pressing down on its back, the slow rhythm of its breath.

"I'm the only thing keeping this world from ending. And I won't let anyone — gods, queens, or emperors — wake it up."

The Queen stared at him for a long moment.

Then she smiled.

It was not a kind smile.

"Bold words, little keeper. But the Emperor's army is descending as we speak. They've tracked the seal's signature to my domain. They'll be here within the hour." She turned, walked back to her throne, sat down like a queen settling into a game she had already won. "And when they arrive

title: The Weight of Ten Thousand Years
content:
The water around Hai Ping turned black.

Not the black of night or shadow. This was the black of absolute emptiness—the color of a place where light had never existed. His lungs burned. His legs kicked uselessly. The pressure crushed against his ears like stone hands.

*You cannot drown.*

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Not sound—not exactly. It resonated through his bones, through the water itself, through the marrow of his existence.

*You are mine now, little Keeper.*

Hai Ping opened his eyes. He should not have been able to see. The darkness was complete. But something *pulled* at his vision, dragged his gaze downward, through layers of impossible depth.

And then he saw it.

The Kun Peng was not a fish. Not a bird. Not a creature that human language could contain.

It stretched across the ocean floor like a mountain range made of shadow. Its scales—if they could be called scales—were the size of temples, each one etched with symbols that predated the first emperor, that predated the Yellow Emperor, that predated the gods themselves. Its eye was a sunken moon, three li across, and it was looking directly at him.

Hai Ping screamed. Bubbles escaped his mouth. Salt water flooded his throat.

But he did not drown.

The Kun Peng's eye shifted. Not a blink—more like the turning of a continent. And Hai Ping felt something break inside his chest, something that had been there since the moment he was born, something he had never known existed until this instant.

His ribs cracked. No—they did not crack. They *opened*.

A light poured from his heart. Golden and terrible, it cut through the black water like a blade through silk. The light touched the Kun Peng's scales, and the great beast *shuddered*.

The ocean floor trembled. Mountains of silt rose in massive clouds. Currents formed and collapsed, spinning Hai Ping in circles until he no longer knew which way was up.

*Ten thousand years,* the voice said. *Ten thousand years since the last seal was placed. Do you know what that means, fisherman's son?*

Hai Ping could not answer. His mouth would not work. His mind would not work. He was a speck of dust before a storm, a single grain of rice in the ocean.

*It means I have been dreaming. And in my dreams, I have seen everything.*

Images flooded his mind.

A woman with jade tears weeping over a battlefield. A man with a bronze face and a thousand arms building a wall of stars. Children dancing in a garden where the flowers sang. A dragon whose body circled the entire world. A city of gold that sank beneath the waves, its towers still glowing in the deep.

The Kun Peng showed him the history of everything, and Hai Ping understood none of it.

*You should not have come.*

The voice was sad now. Heavy. Old in a way that made the mountains feel young.

*The seal was meant to last another thousand years. But the gods grow impatient. They have always wanted my power. They have always feared what I could become.*

Another shudder. The water grew warm. Boiling.

*They are waking me ahead of my time. And when I rise, little Keeper, the oceans will follow.*

Hai Ping felt his body being pushed upward. The light from his chest pulled back into his heart, and his ribs sealed shut. The pressure lessened. The darkness began to fade.

*You cannot stop what is coming. But perhaps... perhaps you can delay it.*

Something touched his forehead. A scale. No larger than his hand, but heavy as a mountain. It pressed against his skin, and the symbols on its surface burned themselves into his memory.

*This is the Seal of Slumber. It has been in my heart for ten thousand years. Now it is in yours. Use it wisely, fisherman's son. Or do not use it at all. The choice is yours.*

The Kun Peng's eye closed.

And Hai Ping shot upward through the water like a cork released from the depths.

---

He broke the surface gasping, coughing, retching salt water onto the deck of a ship he did not recognize.

Strong hands grabbed his arms. Voices shouted. Someone pressed a cloth to his forehead.

"Get him below! Hurry!"

"The seal—did he see—"

"Shut your mouth! Not here, not now!"

Hai Ping's vision swam. The sky above was wrong—streaked with colors that should not exist, purple and green and a red so deep it looked like fresh blood. The sun had vanished. Or maybe it had never been there at all.

He was carried down wooden stairs into darkness. Laid on a cot. Blankets thrown over his shivering body.

A face appeared above him.

Old. Wrinkled like the bark of a pine tree. Eyes that had seen too much and forgotten too little.

"Drink this."

A clay cup pressed against his lips. Bitter liquid flooded his mouth. Hai Ping swallowed, choked, swallowed again.

The old woman watched him with something like pity.

"You saw it, didn't you? The Kun Peng."

Hai Ping tried to speak. His throat was raw, scraped clean by salt and terror.

"The... the fish..."

"It is not a fish." The woman's voice was gentle but firm. "It has never been a fish. The mortals who wrote the Shan Hai Jing did not understand what they were recording. They saw a creature so vast that their minds could not contain it, so they called it a fish and a bird and pretended that was enough."

She sat back, and Hai Ping saw that her robes were not robes at all. They were embroidered with characters that moved, that shifted and writhed like living things.

"My name is Xiu Ying. I am the last of the Keepers before you."

Hai Ping struggled to sit up. "The last—there were others?"

"Eighteen of us. One for each thousand years of the Kun Peng's slumber. The seal passes from Keeper to Keeper, each one chosen by the beast itself." Her eyes grew distant. "I held it for sixty years. I thought I would die with it still in my chest. But the Kun Peng had other plans."

She reached out and touched Hai Ping's forehead. Her fingers were cold, colder than the water had been.

"You are the nineteenth Keeper. And you hold the Seal of Slumber in your heart."

Hai Ping's hand flew to his chest. He felt nothing unusual—just his heartbeat, rapid and terrified.

"It doesn't hurt?"

"The seal is not meant to hurt. It is meant to *hold*. To keep the Kun Peng dreaming, to keep its power contained, to keep the world from drowning in the flood that will come when it wakes."

Xiu Ying's voice dropped to a whisper.

"But the seal is weakening. The gods of heaven have grown tired of waiting. They want the Kun Peng's power for themselves. They have been fighting among themselves for ten thousand years, and now they believe that whoever controls the Great Beast will control the heavens."

Hai Ping's mind raced. The celestial war. The fishing boats that fell from the sky. His father—

"My father," he said, his voice cracking. "Is he—"

"Your father is alive. For now." Xiu Ying's face softened. "He is on this ship. He refused to leave your side, even when the Heavenly Army came for you."

Relief flooded through Hai Ping, so powerful it made him dizzy. But it was quickly replaced by a new fear.

"The Heavenly Army? Why would they come for me?"

"Because you are the Keeper. And the Keeper holds the only thing that can stop the Kun Peng from waking."

Xiu Ying leaned closer. Her breath smelled of ginger and old tea.

"But there is something you must understand, child. The Kun Peng does not want to wake. It has been sleeping for ten thousand years, and it would sleep for ten thousand more if the gods would leave it alone. The Great Beast is not a weapon—it is a prisoner. And you, as its Keeper, are its jailer."

She reached into her robes and pulled out a scroll. Yellowed with age, sealed with wax that glowed with an inner light.

"This is the chronicle of the eighteen Keepers. Their successes. Their failures. The ways they maintained the seal, and the ways they broke."

She pressed it into his hands.

"You must learn from them. You must understand what it means to hold the Kun Peng's sleep in your hands. Because the Heavenly Army is coming. The gods are gathering. And if they find you before you are ready..."

She did not finish the sentence. She did not need to.

Hai Ping looked down at the scroll. The seal pulsed like a heartbeat.

"How long do I have?"

Xiu Ying's face was unreadable.

"The Kun Peng told you itself. You cannot stop what is coming. But perhaps you can delay it."

She stood, her joints creaking.

"Three days. Maybe less. The Heavenly Army has already entered the mortal realm. They know the seal has passed. They know you exist."

She walked toward the door.

"Rest, Keeper. Tomorrow, your education begins."

The door closed behind her.

Hai Ping sat alone in the darkness, the scroll heavy in his hands, the seal burning in his chest, and the weight of ten thousand years pressing down on his shoulders.

Outside, the sky continued to bleed.

title: The Weight of a God's Promise
content:
The Celestial Court did not have walls.

It had clouds that moved like guards, and pillars of jade that stretched into a sky that wasn't really a sky. Hai Ping stood at the edge of a platform that floated above an abyss of stars. Below him, galaxies spun slow and lazy, like fish in a deep pond.

He tried not to look down.

"Stop fidgeting." Bi Fang's voice came from beside him, sharp as broken glass. The bird-woman had not spoken a word during their entire ascent through the clouds. Now she stood with her arms crossed, her red-feathered cloak shifting in a wind that came from nowhere.

"I'm not fidgeting," Hai Ping said. His hands were shaking. He shoved them into his pockets.

"You're thinking about falling."

"I'm thinking about how far it is to the ground."

"There is no ground." Bi Fang's eyes, golden and slit-pupiled, fixed on him. "Only the void between worlds. If you fall, you will fall forever. The gods will watch you shrink to a speck. Then nothing."

"Thanks. That helps."

She almost smiled. Almost.

A horn sounded from somewhere ahead. Deep and resonant, like a whale singing through miles of water. The clouds parted, and Hai Ping saw them.

The gods.

They sat on thrones of carved lightning and fossilized thunder. Some looked like men, but wrong—too tall, too still, their faces carved from stone that had forgotten how to feel. Others were not human at all. A serpent with twelve heads coiled around a pillar, each pair of eyes watching him from a different angle. A woman made of water sat in a floating pool, her form shifting between shapes. A man with antlers of bronze held a spear that crackled with blue fire.

And at the center, on a throne made from a single pearl the size of a fishing boat, sat the Emperor of Heaven.

He looked old. That was Hai Ping's first thought. Old in a way that had nothing to do with years. His face was smooth, his beard black as ink, his robes embroidered with dragons that seemed to move in the corner of the eye. But his eyes—those eyes had seen too much. They held the weight of ten thousand years, and they did not blink.

"Approach, Keeper of the Kun Peng."

The Emperor's voice was soft. It should not have carried across the vast hall. But it did. It pressed against Hai Ping's ears, his chest, his bones.

He walked. One step. Another. The gods watched. The serpent's twelve heads tracked his movement. The water woman rippled. The bronze-antlered man tapped his spear against the floor, a sound like a bell tolling.

Hai Ping stopped ten paces from the throne. He did not bow. He could not. His legs would not obey.

"Your name," the Emperor said.

"Hai Ping."

The Emperor smiled. It was not a kind smile. "The son of a fisherman. From a village so small it does not appear on celestial maps. And yet here you stand, bonded to the oldest creature in existence."

"I didn't ask for it."

"No. You did not." The Emperor leaned forward. The pearl throne groaned. "But the seal chose you. The Kun Peng accepted you. That is not nothing. That is everything."

Silence. The gods waited.

"You will tell me what you saw," the Emperor said. "In the depths. When you touched the creature's mind."

Hai Ping thought of the darkness. The cold that was not cold. The vastness that pressed against his consciousness like an ocean pressing against a paper boat. The voice that had spoken without words.

*Sleep. I am sleeping. Do not wake me.*

"I saw a fish," Hai Ping said. "It was sleeping."

"A fish." The Emperor's smile did not change. "You saw a fish."

"It's the truth."

"The truth." The Emperor stood. He was taller than any man Hai Ping had ever seen. His shadow swallowed the platform. "Do you know what the Kun Peng is, boy? Do you know what it means?"

Hai Ping said nothing.

"The Kun Peng is the boundary between order and chaos. It sleeps, and the world is stable. It wakes, and the world floods. For ten thousand years, it has slept. For ten thousand years, mortals have farmed and fished and died without ever knowing the thing that holds their existence together." The Emperor stepped closer. "And now you, a fisherman's son, have touched its mind. You have claimed it. You have become its Keeper."

"I didn't claim anything."

"You did. The seal does not lie. You carry its mark."

The Emperor raised his hand. Light flickered between his fingers. Hai Ping felt something pull at his chest, at the spot where the seal had burned itself into his skin. He looked down.

The mark was glowing. Blue and silver, pulsing like a heartbeat.

"Interesting," the Emperor said. "It responds to me. But it does not obey me."

"Maybe it doesn't like you."

The words came out before Hai Ping could stop them. The hall went silent. Even the twelve-headed serpent stopped moving.

Bi Fang made a sound like a choked laugh.

The Emperor stared at him. For a long moment, nothing happened. Then the Emperor laughed. It was a terrible sound, like rocks grinding together at the bottom of the sea.

"You have courage," he said. "Or stupidity. Perhaps both. It does not matter. What matters is what happens next."

He turned and walked back to his throne. The pearl groaned as he sat.

"Three days ago, a star fell from the heavens. It struck the mountains west of your village. The impact shattered the peaks and killed every living thing within a hundred li."

Hai Ping's blood went cold.

"Your village was not harmed," the Emperor said. "Not this time. But the star was not a natural occurrence. It was a message. From the Northern Wastes."

"Who sent it?"

"A god who wants the Kun Peng to wake."

The Emperor gestured. The clouds shifted, and an image formed in the air. A man—or what had once been a man. His skin was cracked like dried earth. His eyes were pits of darkness. He stood on a mountain of bones, and behind him, the sky burned green.

"His name is Gong Gong. An ancient god. A god of destruction. He was sealed away three thousand years ago for trying to break the sky. Now he has broken his seal. And he wants the Kun Peng."

"Why?"

"Because if the Kun Peng wakes, the world floods. And in the flood, Gong Gong can reshape creation. Make it in his image. A world of chaos and blood and endless storm."

Hai Ping's throat was dry. "What do you want from me?"

"Keep the Kun Peng asleep."

"Isn't that what I'm doing?"

"No." The Emperor's voice hardened. "You are keeping it calm. There is a difference. The Kun Peng is not a pet. It is not a weapon. It is a force of nature, and you are a boy who happened to touch its mind. If Gong Gong reaches it before you can truly bond with it—if he wakes it with force—the creature will thrash. The oceans will rise. Billions will die."

Hai Ping felt the weight of those words settle on his shoulders. They were heavier than the sky.

"What do I need to do?"

"Train. Learn. Grow." The Emperor waved his hand. "You will remain in the Celestial Court. You will be taught by Bi Fang. You will learn to control the bond. You will learn to speak to the Kun Peng not as a master, but as a friend."

"And if I can't?"

The Emperor's eyes went dark. "Then I will find another way."

He did not explain what that meant. He did not need to.

The audience was over. The gods began to rise. The serpent uncoiled. The water woman dissolved into mist. Bi Fang grabbed Hai Ping's arm and pulled him away.

"Come," she said. "We have work to do."

They walked through corridors of cloud. Past gardens where flowers sang. Past libraries where books flew like birds. Past warriors made of stone who turned their heads to watch him pass.

Hai Ping's mind was spinning. Gong Gong. The star. The flood. Billions dead.

"I can't do this," he said.

Bi Fang stopped. She turned to face him, her golden eyes burning.

"Then you will die. The Kun Peng will wake. And everything you have ever known will be destroyed."

"Thanks for the encouragement."

"I am not encouraging you." She leaned closer. "I am telling you the truth. The Celestial Court does not care about you. They care about the creature. If you fail, they will kill you and bond someone else. Someone stronger. Someone who will not hesitate."

"Then why haven't they done that already?"

"Because the seal chose you. Only you. When you die, the seal will break. It will take a hundred years to form again. A hundred years of chaos, of gods fighting over the power, of mortals dying in wars that are not their own."

She straightened. "So you will not die. You will learn. You will grow. You will become the Keeper the Kun Peng needs."

"I'm a fisherman's son."

"Then learn to fish for gods."

She turned and walked away. Hai Ping stood alone in the corridor of clouds, the mark on his chest still pulsing, the weight of the world pressing down on him like the deep ocean.

He thought of his father. His boat. The nets that had been mended a hundred times.

He thought of the creature sleeping at the bottom of the sea.

And he thought of Gong Gong, the god of destruction, standing on his mountain of bones, waiting for the flood.

Somewhere, deep in the Northern Ocean, the Kun Peng stirred in its sleep.

And in the Celestial Court, a boy who had never wanted to be special learned that some choices are not choices at all.

They are sentences.

Bi Fang was waiting at the end of the corridor. She did not look back. She did not need to.

She knew he would follow.
